steps_raw.instruction: To minimize exogenous RNase contamination, it is recommended to adhereto the followingprocedures: Wear disposable gloves throughout the experiment, and change them frequently.

Laboratory surfaces and equipment should be decontaminated by wiping down with a commercially RNase decontamination solution,a mild solution of bleach, or NaOH, followed by wiping with absolute ethanol, and then rinsing twice with nuclease-free water.
Either decontaminate Glassware and metal ware as above step or bake them at 450 °F ( 230 °C ) for 02:00:00 or more.

Plastic ware should be certified as RNase-free item upon purchasing (such as RNase-free tubes and pipette tips). If used more than one time (for instance slide boxes, slide chambers, as well as staining and dehydration plastic jars), rinse it with absolute ethanol, followed by distilled water, then treat it with the commercial RNase decontamination solution. rinse it again with nuclease-free water, and finally allow it to dry in a hood.

Chemical reagents and solutions are either certified as RNase-freeby the manufacturer, treated with diethylpyrocarbonate (DEPC). In addition, RNase inhibitor, for example SUPERase In™ RNase Inhibitor,ProtectRNA RNaseInhibitor, or RNasin® Plus RNase Inhibitor, could be added to the used solutions. All solutions should not be reused or poured back into their containers.

Dip the slides into the commercial RNase decontamination solution for few seconds. Then, wash two times separately with nuclease-free water. Dry it for 02:00:00 at 37-55 °C . Follow it by UV-treatment shortly before use to destruct any potentially contaminating RNases.

Sacrifice Five-week-old male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats by intraperitoneal injection using 20% Dorminal (200 mg pentobarbital sodium, Alfasan, Woerden-Holland, Netherlands). The dosage is 100 mg per 100 g of body weight. Immediately after animal euthanasia, aseptically remove the mandibular condyles with part of the ramus from the TMJ region. Pay special attention to eliminate any remaining fragments of attached muscles and/or periosteum, however, keep minimal part of the underlying bone (Fig 1). NOTE: Since the MCC of SD rats is very small in size, dissection procedure is performed under light head magnifying glasses (Head magnifier with light Vuemax-Pro, China).

Before starting MCC tissue harvesting, label a cryomold [Tissue-Tek, Biopsy Cryomold (10 x 10 x 5 mm) or (15 x 15 x 5 mm) Sakura Finetek USA Inc., CA, USA], and pour a thin layer of embedding medium [Tissue-Tek, optimal cutting temperature grade (OCT) media, Sakura Finetek USA Inc., CA, USA] on the bottom of it. Place the cryomold in a container filled with dry ice to cool down, and then carry ou tissue harvesting.

Immediately after specimen dissection cool down isopentane (2-methylbutane, Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, USA) by slowly pouring it into a container with dry ice. It is ready for use once the isopentane bubbling subsides.

Collect the dissected specimen, place onto the OCT layer in the cryomold, and orient with RNase-free tweezers so that the region of interest is facing up. NOTE: It is recommend placing both right and left MCC in one cryomold.

Addmore OCT to the mold until the specimen is completely covered. NOTE: Special care should be taken to avoid creating bubbles as cracking may result in later on. These bubbles, if found, could be removed with a pipette tip or a thin needle.

Immediately freeze the prepared cryomold into the cooled isopentane. Upon freezing, the OCT will turn white, this takes a few minutes. NOTE: Pause point : If not proceeding to the next step, the frozen specimen can be stored in the cryomold in a -70 °C freezer.

Set the cryostat (Reichert-Jung Cryocut 1800, Cambridge Instruments GmbH, Nussloch Heidelberger, Germany) temperature to -24 °C to -30 °C . Prepare microslide boxes either plain uncharged or coated charged glass microscope slides as described in the RNAse-free technique above.

Remove the old microtome blade, and wipe down the inside of the cryostat chamber including the antiroll plate and knife holder with 100% ethanol. Then, install a new microtome blade, and transfer the OCT-embedded specimen from the -70 °C freezer to the cryostat to equilibrate.

Treat all metal ware (specimen stage, tweezers), working surfaces and brushes (used to straighten the newly cut sections) as described in the RNAse-free technique above. Then place them inside the cryostat chamber to cool down.

Inside the cryostat chamber, take the frozen OCT-embedded tissue out from the cryomold and mount it securely to the specimen stage with the OCT media. Let the OCT harden, and attach the specimen stage to the cryostat holder.
Place an empty microslide box, but with a small desiccant pack (Desiccant-anhydrous indication Drierite, W.A Hammond Drierite Company LTD, USA) on dry ice near the cryostat.

Start sectioning. First, remove OCT excess by sectioning at 10-20 µm , but when getting close to the tissue set the cutting thickness to 7 µm . Critical Step : Under the recommended settings, smooth tissue sections with minimal tearing and folding will be obtained. This facilitates structural identification of the tissue examined histologically which is crucial for proper LCM application.

Mount the sections onto the central area of a prepared glass microscope slide (HistoBond®+ adhesive microscope slides, Marienfeld laboratory glassware, Lauda-Knigshofen, Germany). NOTE: the slides are kept at room temperature, however, after mounting the first section, the slide should be kept in the cryostat till the remaining sections are cut and placed on it. Critical Step : In the view of sections loss during the next staining and dehydration step, several sections could be attached to one slide, especially that the MCC tissue specimen is small in size. Right and left MCC specimens of the same animal are to be mounted together in one cryomold; thereby one cryosection contains two tissue sections. This enabled us to mount 6 tissue sections (3 cryosections) per slide in a time period much shorter than that needed to cut and mount 6 cryosections. Critical Step : Cutting and mounting should be performed as quickly as possible to avoid RNA degradation. In addition, the sections should not dry on the slide, as this will result in LCM failure because the section will be strongly adhering to the substrate.
As a guide map during LCM procedures, use one cryosection for every tenth slide after staining.

Place the slides with the cryosections in a chilled small-size slide box (enough for 20-25 slides) on dry ice container. When the slide box is full, seal it with laboratory film (Parafilm “M” laboratory film, American National Can, Chicago, IL, USA) and stored at -70 °C . NOTES: If the experiment requires many tissue sections for LCM samples preparation and/or the tissue block is relatively small, as in our case, it is highly recommended to section the whole tissue block at one time and under the same conditions. This also eliminates subjecting the OCT-embedded specimens to multiple freeze-thaw cycles. At the end of the cryosectioning session, approximately 70 slides -each with 6 MCC sections- will be obtained. The sections of the first ten and the last ten slides are very small in size and will not allow reliable identiication of the MCC zones, thus discarded. On average, the tissue sections of the middle 30-40 slides were relatively large and more suitable for histological identiication of the four MCC zones.

To microdissect the tissue successfully, the OCT compound has to be removed and sections have to be stained and dehydrated (or only dehydrated without staining). This step is carried out just before starting the LCM procedure. Stained frozen MCC sections are prepared only for guide map during LCM procedures, while the unstained ones prepared for LCM procedure. The frozen tissue sections, in this protocol, are dehydrated without staining (HistoGene Staining Solution, Arcturus HistoGene‱ LCM Frozen Section Staining Kit, CA, USA) according to the manufacturer's instructions (http:// tools.lifetechnologies.com/content/sfs/manuals/cms 086065 .pdf), but with a few modifications.
After cleaning the staining and dehydration jars as described previously, fill the jars with the RNase-free water and ethanol dehydration series (25 ml/jar) as instructed by the manufacturer.
Remove one slide (with six tissue sections) from the -80 °C freezer and defrost for less than 00:00:30 or until the slide corners have defrosted.
Fill another jar with 100% ethanol, so that sections are dehydrated in absolute ethanol twice.
Add desiccant beads (Molecular sieves 4 beads, Sigma-Aldrich Chemie, Strinheim, Germany) to 100% ethanol jar and xylene jar to prevent rehydration.
To minimize RNase activity, add 50 µL of ProtectRNA (ProtectRNA™ RNase inhibitor 500 x-concentrates, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA) to each jar except the one containing xylene.
Place the first 75% ethanol jar in -20 °C freezer, while the remaining jars are kept On ice or in -4 °C freezer.

Then, with RNase-free forceps transfer the slide from one jar to the next one according to the manufacturer's instructions. Blotting of the excess is needed when transporting the slide between different solutions to avoid the carryover effect. If staining is not to be performed, skip the staining step and the next water wash.
Following immersion of the slide in absolute ethanol, incubate the sections in another 100% ethanol jar to for additional 00:02:00 .

Then continue with the next step (dehydration in xylene and air-drying) as instructed by the manufacturer, and proceed immediately with LCM procedure. NOTE: It is recommended to process one slide at a time in order to complete the staining and dehydration step with the next LCM step as quickly as possible, thereby minimize RNA degradation.
